challenges.title: Print a greeting
challenges.description: Print the text 'Hello, World!' to the console.
challenges.hint: Use the println! macro with a string literal.

steps.title: Understand the entry point
steps.explanation: Every Rust program starts from the main() function. It takes no arguments and returns nothing by default.

steps.title: Print to the console
steps.explanation: println! is a macro (notice the !) that writes a formatted line to standard output.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: Run with `cargo run`. You should see 'Hello, World!' in the terminal.

challenges.title: Sum a vector
challenges.description: Write a function `vec_sum(numbers: &[i32]) -> i32` that returns the sum of all integers in the slice.
challenges.hint: Use the Iterator::sum() method available on slices via .iter().

steps.title: Borrow the slice
steps.explanation: &[i32] is a borrowed slice — a view into a sequence of i32 values. No ownership is transferred.

steps.title: Use iter().sum()
steps.explanation: .iter() creates an iterator over references. .sum() folds the iterator by adding all values.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: vec_sum(&[1, 2, 3]) should return 6. vec_sum(&[]) should return 0.

challenges.title: Count words
challenges.description: Write a function `word_count(text: &str) -> std::collections::HashMap<&str, usize>` that counts how many times each word appears in the input string (words are separated by whitespace).
challenges.hint: Use .split_whitespace() to iterate over words, and the entry().and_modify().or_insert() pattern to update counts.

steps.title: Split on whitespace
steps.explanation: .split_whitespace() handles any run of whitespace and skips leading/trailing spaces.

steps.title: Update the map
steps.explanation: entry(word).or_insert(0) returns a mutable reference to the count, inserting 0 if absent. Dereferencing and incrementing updates it in place.

steps.title: Lifetimes
steps.explanation: The 'a lifetime ties the keys in the HashMap to the input &str, so they stay valid as long as the original string lives.
steps.code: fn word_count<'a>(text: &'a str) -> HashMap<&'a str, usize> { ... }

challenges.title: Generic stack with trait bound
challenges.description: Implement a generic `Stack<T>` where T implements the `Display` trait. It must support `push(&mut self, item: T)`, `pop(&mut self) -> Option<T>`, and `peek(&self) -> Option<&T>`. Also implement a `print_all` method that prints every element.
challenges.hint: Back the stack with a Vec<T>. Vec already has push and pop. Use last() for peek.

steps.title: Back with Vec
steps.explanation: Vec<T> already handles dynamic sizing and provides push() and pop() with the exact semantics a stack needs.

steps.title: Trait bound: Display
steps.explanation: The T: Display bound in impl<T: Display> means every method in this block can call println!("{}", item) on a T.

steps.title: peek returns a reference
steps.explanation: Option<&T> borrows from the Vec — no cloning or ownership transfer needed. Vec::last() returns this for free.
